sentence: The patient, Betty, is a 30-year-old female who presents with a history of asthma, complaining of frequent exacerbations, typically triggered by respiratory infections, and occasional exposure to her aunt's cat.
She reports using a blue inhaler, identified as Ventolin (albuterol), which provides relief but is used almost daily.
She has had multiple urgent care and emergency department visits over the past six months, often requiring antibiotics, steroids, or hospitalization.
She denies smoking, vaping, or marijuana use and has no history of seasonal allergies.
Vital signs: blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam reveals diminished lung sounds with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally, and strong bilateral pulses with brisk capillary refill.
Assessment: The patient has moderate intermittent asthma, consistent with pulmonary function test results.
Problem list: Asthma; Asthma, moderate intermittent; Asthma, suspected; Asthma, past.
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms one puff twice per day to prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for albuterol inhaler use.
I will also provide an asthma action plan to manage symptoms and exacerbations.
I instructed the patient to continue using Ventolin inhaler as needed, two puffs every 4-6 hours.
I will see the patient in a month or sooner if symptoms worsen.
